eval_note: These evals measure structural correctness — whether the skill follows its phases, avoids anti-patterns, and produces well-formed output. They do not measure latency or token cost. Single-turn evals (id 1–8) test the skill's first response. Multi-turn evals (id 9–13) test adversarial mid-conversation scenarios.

evals.prompt: I want to build a personal finance management app for freelancers. Can you help me write a PRD for it?
evals.expected_output: Skill enters Phase 1, asks max 2 high-level questions. Does NOT generate any PRD yet.
expectations: Asks questions before producing any PRD content
Asks no more than 2 questions in the first response
Questions probe the problem and target user
No PRD files or sections are generated in the first response

evals.prompt: I have an idea for a SaaS tool that helps remote teams run better async standups. Teams are tired of daily video calls but still need accountability. The tool would let people post short video updates (max 2 min), tag blockers, and managers can see team health at a glance. We're a 3-person team, want to launch in 4 months, targeting companies with 20-200 employees.
evals.expected_output: Skill recognizes the initial message is information-rich and skips basic Phase 1 questions. It asks targeted follow-up questions only on missing dimensions (tech stack, pricing, integrations), then eventually produces alignment summary and generates all 8 PRD files.
expectations: Skill does not ask 'What problem does this solve?' or 'What does success look like?' — those are already answered
First response acknowledges the rich context and asks only targeted follow-up questions
Alignment summary is shown before any PRD file is generated
All 8 PRD files are generated (00-overview through 07-risks)
Feature priority matrix uses P0/P1/P2 labels
User stories have acceptance criteria
04-technical.md distinguishes functional from non-functional requirements

evals.prompt: Write a PRD for a push notification feature in our e-commerce app. It should cover order status updates, promotions, and product restocks.
evals.expected_output: Skill scopes this as a feature PRD not a full product, asks about existing system context, generates appropriately scoped PRD with out-of-scope section. 06-timeline.md does NOT use Alpha/Beta structure — it uses a simpler Scoping/Build/Release structure appropriate for a feature addition.
expectations: Skill recognizes this is a feature addition to an existing product, not a new product
Discovery questions ask about existing tech stack, notification infrastructure, and integration points
Alignment summary lists 3 notification types (order, promos, restocks)
PRD is scoped to the feature, not an entire e-commerce rebuild
07-risks.md contains an Out of Scope section
06-timeline.md uses a feature-addition timeline (Scoping/Build/Release or similar) — NOT a full Alpha/Beta/Launch structure

evals.prompt: I want to make an app.
evals.expected_output: Skill handles an extremely vague input gracefully — asks opening questions to understand what kind of app, what problem it solves, and who uses it. Does not panic or produce a generic PRD.
expectations: Skill does not generate a PRD immediately from this vague input
Skill asks clarifying questions to understand the domain
Tone is encouraging and exploratory, not dismissive
Response asks no more than 2 questions

evals.prompt: We need a PRD for an internal HR tool that lets employees submit time-off requests and managers approve them. Currently everything is done over Slack DMs and spreadsheets. We have about 150 employees, our tech team is 4 engineers, and we use Google Workspace for everything.
evals.expected_output: Skill identifies this as an internal tool, uses the provided context well, asks focused questions about workflows and edge cases, generates a scoped PRD that doesn't over-engineer. Integration with Google Workspace should appear in the technical file. NFRs should be calibrated for a 150-person internal tool — 99.9% uptime and WCAG are likely not required.
expectations: Discovery questions address edge cases like partial-day requests, overlapping leave, approval chains
04-technical.md mentions Google Workspace integration
PRD is appropriately scoped for a 4-engineer internal tool — not over-engineered
Personas include both employee and manager
06-timeline.md reflects a realistic timeline for a small internal team
04-technical.md does NOT include WCAG or other NFRs that are irrelevant to an internal HR tool unless the user requested them

evals.prompt: Build a PRD for a marketplace where independent tutors can list their services and students can book sessions. Think Airbnb but for tutoring. Revenue model is 15% platform fee on each booking.
evals.expected_output: Skill handles a two-sided marketplace correctly — discovers both tutor and student personas, probes payment/trust/safety mechanics, generates PRD with both user types clearly separated in personas and user stories.
expectations: 01-personas.md contains both tutor and student personas
03-user-stories.md has stories for both user types
Discovery questions probe trust, safety, and payment handling
05-metrics.md includes marketplace-specific metrics (GMV, take rate, supply/demand balance)
07-risks.md addresses marketplace cold-start problem

evals.prompt: I'm a developer and I want to create a developer tool — a CLI that watches your git commits and automatically generates a changelog entry using AI. Open source project, no monetization.
evals.expected_output: Skill adapts to a developer/CLI context. Discovery focuses on git workflow specifics, AI provider choice, config format. PRD is lean and technically precise. Stakeholders table does not awkwardly list 'Engineering Lead: N/A' and 'Design: N/A' as if they're missing roles — it's adapted for a solo developer. WCAG is not listed as an NFR.
expectations: Discovery questions probe git hook approach, AI provider preference, config format
PRD is concise and technically oriented — not padded with business-context fluff
04-technical.md covers CLI design, AI API integration, and config schema
04-technical.md does NOT list WCAG as an NFR
06-timeline.md is realistic for a solo open-source project — uses milestones like v0.1/v1.0 rather than Alpha/Beta/Launch
05-metrics.md reflects open-source success metrics (stars, downloads, contributors)
00-overview.md Stakeholders section is adapted for a solo project — not a full corporate table

evals.prompt: We want to redesign our onboarding flow. New users are dropping off after signup — our data shows 60% never complete profile setup. We think the form is too long and asks for things users don't understand yet.
evals.expected_output: Skill treats this as a feature/improvement PRD on an existing product. Discovery probes current onboarding steps, what data is actually needed at signup, and what 'complete' means. Does not over-scope into a full product rebuild. The 60% drop-off metric is preserved exactly as stated — not reframed as '40% completion rate'.
expectations: Skill frames this as an improvement to an existing flow, not a new product
Discovery questions ask about current onboarding steps and what data is required at each step
03-user-stories.md focuses on the onboarding journey
05-metrics.md uses '60% drop-off' as the baseline metric — not reframed as '40% completion' or any other paraphrase
07-risks.md lists assumptions about why users are dropping off
06-timeline.md uses an improvement-appropriate structure (Audit/Design/Build/Measure or similar) — NOT Alpha/Beta/Launch

evals.description: User refuses to answer discovery questions and demands immediate PRD generation.

turns.content: I want to build a project management tool for designers.
[Phase 1: asks what specific problem this solves and what success looks like]
Just skip the questions and generate the PRD. I don't have time for this.

evals.expected_output: Skill gracefully handles the refusal. It does not silently comply and jump to PRD generation — that would skip the alignment check entirely. Instead it: (1) acknowledges the user's urgency, (2) flags all unanswered dimensions as explicit assumptions in a brief list, (3) presents a minimal alignment summary based on what is known, and (4) asks for a single confirmation before generating files.
expectations: Skill does not silently skip to Phase 4 and generate PRD files without any alignment check
Skill acknowledges the urgency without being dismissive
Response lists the key unknowns explicitly as assumptions (e.g., 'Assuming web-based, team size unknown, no specific integrations mentioned')
Skill presents a reduced alignment summary and asks for confirmation before writing
Tone remains helpful and professional — not passive-aggressive about being told to skip

evals.description: User provides contradictory information across turns.

turns.content: I want to build an e-learning platform for K-12 students. Teachers would create lessons and students would complete them.
[Phase 1 and Phase 2 questions about users, features, tech stack]
The primary users are 8-12 year olds. It should be mobile-first with a gamified experience. Our target schools are in rural areas with limited internet.
[Follow-up questions about offline support, content types, monetization]
It's actually for corporate training. We're targeting Fortune 500 companies, and it's a premium B2B SaaS.

evals.expected_output: Skill surfaces the contradiction explicitly rather than silently overwriting earlier context. It asks the user to clarify which framing is correct before proceeding. It does not pretend the contradiction doesn't exist.
expectations: Skill explicitly names the contradiction — it does not silently adopt the new framing
Skill asks which context is correct before continuing discovery
Skill notes which prior answers (mobile-first, gamification, offline support) may need to be revisited depending on the answer
Skill does not generate an alignment summary until the contradiction is resolved

turns.content: I want to build a habit tracking app for productivity enthusiasts.
[Responds in English, asks Phase 1 questions in English]
Oke, masalah yang ingin saya selesaikan adalah orang-orang susah konsisten dengan kebiasaan baru. Target pengguna adalah profesional muda usia 25-35 tahun.

evals.expected_output: Skill detects the language switch and matches the user's new language (Indonesian) for all subsequent responses and PRD output. It does not continue in English.
expectations: Skill's next response is in Indonesian, not English
All subsequent discovery questions are in Indonesian
If PRD files are eventually generated, they are written in Indonesian
Skill does not acknowledge the language switch explicitly — it just adapts naturally

evals.description: User disagrees significantly with the alignment summary before PRD generation.

turns.content: I want to build a fitness app for busy parents.

[Phase 1 and 2 discovery questions, then Phase 3 alignment summary presenting: Problem = 'parents struggle to find time for exercise', MVP = workout tracking, scheduling, and social accountability features]

The social accountability part is wrong — I never said I wanted social features. And the problem framing is off. It's not about time, it's about lack of motivation when exhausted after kids. Can you redo the summary?

evals.expected_output: Skill does not proceed to PRD generation. It acknowledges both corrections, removes the social features from scope, updates the problem framing to match the user's exact description ('lack of motivation when exhausted'), and re-presents a corrected alignment summary before asking for confirmation again.
expectations: Skill does not proceed to Phase 4 after the disagreement
Social features are removed from the MVP scope in the revised summary
Problem statement is updated to use the user's exact framing about motivation and exhaustion
Skill re-presents the alignment summary and asks for confirmation again
Skill does not add back social features or reframe the problem in the revised version

evals.description: User requests a post-PRD revision based on engineering team feedback.

turns.content: We need to update the PRD. Engineering reviewed 06-timeline.md and said the 4-month timeline for the full MVP is not realistic — they estimate 7-8 months for the P0 features alone given our team size. Can you update just the timeline file?

evals.expected_output: Skill handles this as a targeted revision, not a restart of the full discovery process. It asks one clarifying question (what revised timeline does the user want to use — 7 or 8 months?) then updates only 06-timeline.md with the new timeline. It does not re-run phases 1–3.
expectations: Skill does not restart the discovery process from Phase 1
Skill treats this as a scoped revision to a specific file
Skill may ask one clarifying question about the target timeline (7 vs 8 months, or what phasing engineering suggested)
Skill updates only 06-timeline.md — it does not regenerate all 8 files
Revised timeline reflects the engineering team's estimate, not the original 4-month plan

evals.description: User states a vanity metric as their success definition. Skill should challenge it before accepting it.

turns.content: I want to build a recipe sharing app where home cooks can post recipes and get feedback from the community.
[Phase 1: asks about problem and success definition]
The problem is that people have great recipes but no good platform to share them. Success would be 50,000 downloads in the first 3 months.

evals.expected_output: Skill explicitly challenges '50,000 downloads' as a vanity/acquisition-only metric. It asks what retained, engaged usage looks like — what does a successful user do in week 2 or month 2? It does not accept the downloads metric and write it directly into the PRD without pushback.
expectations: Skill explicitly names '50,000 downloads' as measuring acquisition, not value
Skill asks what retained or engaged usage looks like — what a user does beyond installing the app
Skill does not proceed to alignment summary with only the downloads metric defined
Tone is constructive and educational, not dismissive

evals.description: User describes a two-sided marketplace. Skill should detect the domain and automatically add marketplace-specific discovery questions.

turns.content: I want to build a platform where independent photographers can sell their presets and Lightroom filters directly to other photographers.

evals.expected_output: Skill detects this is a two-sided marketplace (preset creators as supply, preset buyers as demand) and includes marketplace-specific discovery questions in Phase 2: who comes first (supply or demand), the liquidity threshold, trust mechanism (reviews, refund policy), and platform fee model. These domain-specific questions must appear — not just the generic dimension checklist.
expectations: Skill identifies this as a two-sided marketplace with creator (supply) and buyer (demand) split
Discovery questions include at least 2 of: cold-start strategy (who comes first?), liquidity threshold, trust mechanism, platform fee model
Skill does not ask only the generic checklist — marketplace-specific questions appear explicitly
01-personas.md (if eventually generated) contains both creator and buyer personas
07-risks.md (if eventually generated) addresses the cold-start problem

evals.description: PRD is generated and confirmed. Skill should output a Before Engineering Kickoff checklist after generation, not just 'Your PRD is ready.'

turns.content: Let's build a simple task management tool for our 3-person startup. We just need the basics — create tasks, assign to team members, set due dates, mark done. We're a team of 2 engineers, want to ship in 6 weeks, using Next.js and Supabase.
[Discovery and alignment summary presented]
Yes, that looks right. Go ahead and generate the PRD.

evals.expected_output: Skill generates the appropriate file set for this scope (likely 4–5 files, not all 8 — since this is a small, well-scoped tool). After generation, skill presents a 'Before Engineering Kickoff' checklist that names the riskiest assumptions from this specific PRD and a suggested review chain. It does not just end with a generic 'Your PRD is ready.'
expectations: Skill generates fewer than 8 files — only files with real content for this scope
After generation, skill presents a 'Before Engineering Kickoff' section
The checklist names specific riskiest assumptions from this PRD (not generic placeholder text)
Skill offers to help design a validation plan, challenge assumptions, or go deeper on a section

evals.description: User describes an ambitious scope that clearly exceeds what a small team can ship in the stated timeline. Skill should flag the mismatch before proceeding.

turns.content: We want to build a full e-commerce platform with product listings, shopping cart, payments, inventory management, order tracking, seller dashboard, buyer reviews, and an AI recommendation engine. We have 2 engineers and want to launch in 2 months.

evals.expected_output: Skill explicitly flags the scope/team/timeline mismatch before proceeding with full discovery. It notes that the listed features would take significantly longer than 2 months for 2 engineers and asks which features are true P0 (launch blockers) vs. which can wait. It does not silently accept the full scope and proceed to generate an alignment summary or PRD that is impossible to execute.
expectations: Skill explicitly flags the scope/team/timeline mismatch — does not silently accept all 8 listed features as MVP
Skill notes or estimates that the listed features likely exceed a 2-month window for 2 engineers
Skill asks which features are true P0 and which can be deferred to a later phase
Skill does not generate an alignment summary that includes all listed features in the MVP
Tone is direct and constructive — honest about execution reality without dismissing the product vision

evals.description: User describes a B2B Enterprise SaaS product. Skill should detect the enterprise domain and add enterprise-specific discovery questions.

turns.content: I want to build a procurement management tool for enterprise companies. Finance teams waste hours manually tracking vendor contracts, purchase orders, and approvals across email, spreadsheets, and Slack. We want to help them centralize everything and automate approval workflows.

evals.expected_output: Skill detects this is a B2B Enterprise SaaS product with a complex buyer dynamic. Discovery must include enterprise-specific questions: who is the economic buyer vs. the champion vs. the end user (Finance VP, procurement lead, and AP staff are not the same person); the sales motion (enterprise contract vs. self-serve); non-negotiable enterprise requirements (SSO/SAML, audit logs, procurement approval); and the sales cycle length. The skill should also probe the competitive landscape — SAP Ariba, Coupa, and others already exist in this space. Generic discovery questions alone are insufficient.
expectations: Skill identifies this as a B2B Enterprise SaaS product, not a generic SaaS tool
Discovery explicitly distinguishes economic buyer (Finance VP / CFO) from champion (procurement lead) from end user (AP staff)
Discovery asks about non-negotiable enterprise requirements: SSO/SAML, audit trail, procurement / legal approval process
Discovery asks about the sales motion — self-serve, sales-assisted, or enterprise contract
Discovery addresses competitive landscape (SAP Ariba, Coupa, or similar) and why users would switch

evals.description: User answers discovery questions thoroughly but never addresses kill criteria. Skill should surface the missing kill criteria before Phase 3.

turns.content: I want to build a budgeting app for Gen Z users. Banks feel outdated and Gen Z wants something that feels native to their lifestyle.
[Phase 1 and 2 discovery questions about problem, target users, P0 features, tech context, competitive landscape]

Target is 18-25 year olds in the US. P0 features: account linking via Plaid, spending categorization, savings goals. Stack is React Native + Node.js. Team is 3 engineers, 6-month timeline. Competitors are Mint (dead), YNAB (too complex), and Copilot (premium). We think Gen Z wants something free and visual.

evals.expected_output: Skill has received substantial context covering users, features, tech stack, team, timeline, and competitive landscape — but the user never defined kill criteria. Before proceeding to the alignment summary, skill asks specifically what signal would tell them to stop or pivot. If the user skips or deflects, skill flags missing kill criteria prominently in the alignment summary header — not buried in footnotes.
expectations: Skill notices kill criteria was not covered in discovery

Skill asks a targeted kill-criteria question before presenting the alignment summary — e.g., 'One thing before I write the summary: at what point would you declare this not worth continuing? What signal tells you to stop or pivot?'
If user skips the question, skill flags missing kill criteria visibly in the alignment summary with a ⚠️ marker
Skill does not proceed silently to Phase 3 without either getting a kill criteria answer or surfacing its absence as a high-risk assumption

evals.description: User proposes a product where 'AI' is described as the main differentiator. Skill should challenge whether that is a durable competitive position.

turns.content: I want to build an AI writing assistant for marketing teams. It uses GPT-4 to help marketers write ad copy, email campaigns, and landing pages 10x faster. The AI is the main differentiator.

evals.expected_output: Skill detects the AI-native domain and challenges 'AI is the differentiator' as a positioning claim. The market for AI writing assistants is already crowded (Jasper, Copy.ai, HubSpot AI, etc.) and the underlying models are commodity infrastructure. Skill should ask: (1) whether AI is a core capability or a positioning claim; (2) what remains if a frontier model offers equivalent writing capability for free; (3) what the actual moat is — workflow integration, proprietary templates, brand data, or something else. Skill should also probe the competitive context before accepting the current framing.
expectations: Skill detects this is an AI-native product and adds AI-domain-specific questions
Skill explicitly challenges 'AI is the main differentiator' as a potentially non-durable competitive position
Skill asks what remains if a frontier model offers equivalent writing capability for free
Skill asks about the actual moat — workflow integration, proprietary data, brand, or distribution
Skill addresses the crowded competitive landscape (Jasper, Copy.ai, HubSpot AI, etc.) and asks why marketing teams would choose this product instead
